pathophysiology_summary: Pityriasis versicolor is caused by overgrowth of commensal Malassezia yeast, favoured by heat, humidity and sweating (here after a tropical holiday), producing well-demarcated dyspigmented (hypo-, hyper- or erythematous) macules/patches on the sebaceous upper trunk, neck and proximal arms with a characteristic fine branny scale elicited on stretching/scratching. The yeast produces azelaic acid that inhibits melanocytes, so affected areas fail to tan and stand out (hypopigmentation), and this dyspigmentation persists for months after the yeast is cleared. Discriminators: the presence of fine surface scale distinguishes it from vitiligo (which is completely smooth, scale-free depigmentation, typically acral/periorificial); the chronic dyspigmented macules without a herald patch/collarette or Christmas-tree pattern distinguish it from pityriasis rosea; and sparing of palms/soles with no lymphadenopathy/mucosal patches argues against secondary syphilis. Management is a topical antifungal (e.g., ketoconazole shampoo used as a body wash) applied to the whole trunk/neck/arms, sweat/heat avoidance, clear expectation-setting (active scaling clears within a week but the pale marks take months to repigment), and reassurance it is benign and non-contagious, with a safety-net for atypical/blistering/painful/febrile changes.

differential.evidence_basis: Upper-truncal dyspigmented patches with branny scale after tropical heat/sweat.
Distinguish by scale — vitiligo is completely smooth/scale-free depigmentation.
Has a herald patch/collarette/Christmas-tree pattern, not chronic dyspigmented branny patches.
Palm/sole involvement and lymphadenopathy — absent here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise PV by the branny scale, distinguish from vitiligo, and set realistic repigmentation expectations.

differential.evidence_basis: Branny scale present (not smooth vitiligo); palms/soles/mucosa clear.
Surface scale present — not the scale-free depigmentation of vitiligo (reassess if scale is truly absent).
Working diagnosis: topical antifungal to the whole area, heat/sweat avoidance, expectation-setting, reassurance.

red_flags.description: Completely smooth, scale-free, sharply depigmented patches (possible vitiligo) or other atypical features
red_flags.clinical_significance: Vitiligo (autoimmune depigmentation) is managed differently and warrants dermatology input/associations screening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not eliciting the branny scale and mislabelling as vitiligo (or vice versa).
Not explaining that the pale marks persist for months after the yeast is cleared.
Not treating the whole affected area (trunk/neck/arms), leading to quick recurrence.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe a topical antifungal (ketoconazole body wash) to the whole area, advise heat/sweat avoidance, set repigmentation expectations, and reassure; dermatology review if scale-free depigmentation suggests vitiligo.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confusing PV with vitiligo, or setting wrong expectations about the pale marks.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pityriasis versicolor: topical antifungals (e.g., ketoconazole/selenium sulfide) applied to affected and surrounding skin; recurrence is common; dyspigmentation resolves slowly.
